chunks.text: Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems Center (iCyPhy) Skip to main content iCyPhy Home Conferences Links Membership Project Personnel Publications Resources Sponsors Wikis Skip to sidebar Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems Center (iCyPhy) This website is

-Physical Systems Center (iCyPhy) This website is obsolete! Please see icyphy.org Events: Mini-Workshop , held February 14, 2019. The goal of iCyPhy (pronounced eye-sigh-fie) is to conduct pre-competitive research on architectures and design, m

tive research on architectures and design, modeling, and analysis techniques for cyber-physical systems, with emphasis on industrial applications. Cyber-physical systems integrate computing, networking, and physical components. Appl

g, networking, and physical components. Applications include transportation systems, automation, security, smart buildings, smart cities, medical systems, energy generation and distribution, water distribution, agriculture, military

n, water distribution, agriculture, military systems, process control, asset management, and robotics. The CPS intellectual challenge is about the intersection, not the union, of the physical and the cyber. This intersection combines engi

d the cyber. This intersection combines engineering models and methods from mechanical, environmental, civil, electrical, biomedical, chemical, aeronautical and industrial engineering with the models and methods of computer science

with the models and methods of computer science and engineering. iCyPhy research is founded on the conviction that these models and methods do not combine easily, and that consequently CPS constitutes a new engineering discipline that d

PS constitutes a new engineering discipline that demands its own models and methods. ICyPhy is a collaborative effort between academia in the EECS Department at UC Berkeley and industry. ICyPhy member companies provide financial support for research

companies provide financial support for research projects, interact collaboratively with researchers, and commercialize research results. ICyPhy is committed to open publication and open-source release of software and hardware designs us

release of software and hardware designs using a BSD-style license and will not seek IP protection for research results unless there is an overwhelmingly compelling case that IP protection is necessary for the results to have

tection is necessary for the results to have impact. Specific current research directions include: Model-based design of cyber-physical systems. Highly dynamic networked systems (lifetime management, connectivity, adaptation). The Internet of t

ment, connectivity, adaptation). The Internet of things (IoT), swarm systems, edge computing, and smart gateways. Safety, privacy, and security for IoT. Synthesis and learning for cyber-physical system design and adaptation. Localization and location

m design and adaptation. Localization and location-aware services. Software and network architectures for heterogeneous distributed IoT applications. Integration of learning and optimization into safety-critical systems. Human-in-the-loop systems. Sy

ty-critical systems. Human-in-the-loop systems. Systems-of-systems design. Semantics of timed systems. Projects: Reactors, deterministic reactive components Lingua Franca , a coordination language for defining and composing reactors Accessors , a fra

defining and composing reactors Accessors , a framework for programming the Internet of Things Cape Code , a development environment and host for applications that use accessors. CapeCode is a configuration of Ptolemy II. Distributed discrete-event

guration of Ptolemy II. Distributed discrete-event simulation using HLA , a collaboration with ISAE, Toulouse Board of Directors: Prabal Dutta (Associate Professor, University of California, Berkeley) Edward A. Lee (Robert S. Pepper Distinguished Pro

Edward A. Lee (Robert S. Pepper Distinguished Professor, University of California, Berkeley) Alberto Sangiovanni-Vincentelli (Edgar L. and Harold H. Buttner Chair Professor, University of California, Berkeley) Sanjit Seshia (Professor, University of
